stories_covered: Harvey AI legal platform raises $200M at $11B valuation
SaaS apocalypse narrative vs. actual market performance
Atlassian growth and RPO acceleration despite AI concerns
Revenue multiple expansion and TAM expansion debate
Consulting services growth in AI implementation era
harry_takes: Software as a category is not dead—the industry continues normal creative destruction cycles
Revenue growth path matters more than TAM projections; let the market show you the way
Product and engineering are above the fold in AI era while support and back-office face cuts
Margin stacking between model providers, cloud platforms, and SaaS creates complex revenue allocation
harry_boldest_quote: The idea that software as a category is dead is ludicrous to me.
predictions: Lemkin: Companies like Atlassian will continue to grow if they invest in AI-powered products
Cannon-Brooks: Every five years some competitors disappear but winners keep emerging; architectural shifts cause higher churn
Harry: Most SaaS businesses will be fine but some will dramatically underperform if they fail to integrate AI
Lemkin: Consulting spend will grow not shrink as enterprises need FDEs to implement AI solutions
disagreements: Yes—whether SaaS is broadly threatened or just experiencing normal category rotation. Lemkin and Cannon-Brooks argue SaaS infrastructure will remain critical; Harry suggests differentiation between above-fold (product/engineering) and below-fold (support, admin) categories.
harry_role: Moderates between optimism (software survives) and pessimism (software dying), synthesizes Cannon-Brooks' grounded Atlassian perspective, pushes back on zero-sum thinking about TAM capture.
notable: Mike Cannon-Brooks provides crucial enterprise reality-check: companies using multiple AI models in production, integrating them via cost-aware gateways, paying AWS then Anthropic in margin stacks—far more nuanced than headline revenue figures suggest.
